data:
Add a published contract package as a dependency in your soroban.toml.
soropkg add <package> [flags]
soropkg resolves the requested semver range against the registry, records the resolved version in your manifest, and fetches the contract spec from the network.
data.arguments#Arguments: Argument	Required	Description	<package>	Yes	Package name with optional version range, e.g. blend-capital/blend@^2.0.0
data.flags#Flags: Flag	Description	--mainnet	Resolve against mainnet contracts (default)	--testnet	Resolve against testnet contracts	--futurenet	Resolve against futurenet contracts	--dev	Add to [dev-dependencies] instead of [dependencies]
data.version-syntax#Version syntax:
soropkg uses standard semver ranges:
soropkg add stellar/token@1.0.0      # exact version
soropkg add blend-capital/blend@^2   # compatible with 2.x.x
soropkg add soroswap/router@~1.2     # compatible with 1.2.x
soropkg add phoenix/pair             # latest published version
data.example#Example:
soropkg add blend-capital/blend@^2.0.0
Resolving blend-capital/blend@^2.0.0...
  ✓  Resolved blend-capital/blend@2.0.1
  ✓  Updated soroban.toml
Your manifest is updated:
[dependencies]
"blend-capital/blend" = "^2.0.0"
After adding dependencies, run soropkg generate to regenerate TypeScript clients for the new packages.

data:
Generate fully-typed TypeScript client bindings for every package in your [dependencies].
soropkg generate [flags]
data.what-gets-generated#What gets generated:
For each dependency, soropkg generates a .ts file in .soroban/ containing:
A Client class with one method per contract function
Full TypeScript types for all arguments and return values, derived directly from the on-chain contract spec
A ClientOptions interface for passing RPC URL, keypair, and network passphrase
data.flags#Flags: Flag	Description	--mainnet	Use mainnet contract addresses (default)	--testnet	Use testnet contract addresses	--out <dir>	Output directory (default: .soroban/)	--package <name>	Only generate for a specific package
data.example#Example:
soropkg generate
  ✓  stellar__token.ts
  ✓  blend_capital__blend.ts
  ✓  blend_capital__backstop.ts
Written to .soroban/
data.file-naming#File naming:
Generated files use the pattern {org}__{package}.ts, with / replaced by __:
Package name	Generated file	stellar/token	stellar__token.ts	blend-capital/blend	blend_capital__blend.ts	soroswap/router	soroswap__router.ts	
Add .soroban/ to your .gitignore — these files should be generated at build time, not committed to source control.

data:
soropkg is a command-line tool. All commands follow the pattern:
soropkg <command> [arguments] [flags]
data.commands#Commands: Command	Description	inspect	Read the interface of a deployed contract	init	Initialise a soroban.toml manifest	add	Add a contract dependency	publish	Publish a package to the registry	generate	Generate TypeScript clients for dependencies	search	Search the registry
data.global-flags#Global flags:
These flags are available on every command:
Flag	Description	--mainnet	Use Stellar mainnet (default)	--testnet	Use Stellar testnet	--futurenet	Use Stellar futurenet	--verbose, -v	Enable verbose output	--help, -h	Show help	--version	Print version
data.configuration#Configuration: soropkg reads configuration from soroban.toml in the current working directory. See soroban.toml reference for the full schema.

data:
Scaffold a soroban.toml manifest for your project interactively.
soropkg init [flags]
data.flags#Flags: Flag	Description	--yes, -y	Skip prompts and generate with defaults
data.interactive-prompts#Interactive prompts:
Running soropkg init without flags will walk you through:
? Package name (org/name): my-org/my-protocol
? Version: 1.0.0
? Description: My Soroban protocol
? License: Apache-2.0
? Repository URL: https://github.com/my-org/my-protocol
✓  Created soroban.toml
data.generated-file#Generated file:
[package]
name        = "my-org/my-protocol"
version     = "1.0.0"
description = "My Soroban protocol"
license     = "Apache-2.0"
repository  = "https://github.com/my-org/my-protocol"
# Add your deployed contract addresses here
[networks.mainnet]
# vault = "CC..."
[networks.testnet]
# vault = "CC..."
[dependencies]
# "stellar/token" = "1.0.0"
See the soroban.toml reference for the full schema including all supported fields.

data:
Read the live interface of any deployed Soroban contract directly from the blockchain.
soropkg inspect <contract-address> [flags]
soropkg fetches the WASM binary from the network, parses the embedded contractspecv0 section, and prints a structured view of the contract's public interface — functions, argument types, return types, and custom error codes.No ABI files or separate metadata needed.
data.arguments#Arguments: Argument	Required	Description	<contract-address>	Yes	The C... Stellar contract address to inspect
data.flags#Flags: Flag	Description	--mainnet	Inspect on mainnet (default)	--testnet	Inspect on testnet	--futurenet	Inspect on futurenet	--json	Output raw JSON instead of formatted text
data.example#Example:
soropkg inspect 
Contract   blend-capital/backstop
Network    mainnet
WASM hash  
Functions
  deposit(from: Address, pool: Address, amount: i128) → i128
  withdraw(from: Address, pool: Address, amount: i128) → i128
  queue_withdrawal(from: Address, pool: Address, amount: i128)
  dequeue_withdrawal(from: Address, pool: Address, amount: i128)
  claim(from: Address, pool_addresses: Vec<Address>) → i128
Errors
  1  InvalidShareMintAmount
  2  InvalidTokenWithdrawAmount
  3  InsufficientFunds
  4  NotExpired

data:
Publish your contract package to the soropkg registry so others can install it.
soropkg publish [flags]
data.authenticate-with-github#Authenticate wi: soropkg uses GitHub OAuth to verify package ownership. On first publish, your browser opens a GitHub auth page. Once authorised, ownership is tied to your GitHub org/username — which must match the name prefix in your soroban.toml.
data.verify-on-chain#Verify on-chain: soropkg fetches the live WASM from the network addresses declared in your manifest and computes its hash. This hash is stored in the registry alongside your package — ensuring that what you publish matches what is actually deployed.
data.publish-to-registry#Publish to registry: Your manifest, contract specs, and verified WASM hashes are submitted to the registry. The package becomes discoverable via soropkg search and installable via soropkg add.
data.requirements#Requirements:
Before publishing:
A valid soroban.toml with at least one network's contract addresses
GitHub authentication (org/username must match package name prefix)
Contracts must be deployed and accessible on the specified network
data.flags#Flags: Flag	Description	--mainnet	Publish mainnet contract addresses (default)	--testnet	Publish testnet contract addresses	--dry-run	Validate and verify without actually publishing	--tag <tag>	Add a release tag (e.g. stable, beta)
data.example#Example:
soropkg publish
Reading soroban.toml...
Authenticating via GitHub...  ✓
Verifying contracts on mainnet:
  vault  CCVAULTMAINNET...  ✓  hash: 8a3f...c221
Publishing my-org/my-protocol@1.0.0...  ✓
Package available at:
  soropkg add my-org/my-protocol@1.0.0
data.versioning#Versioning:
Use standard semver in your soroban.toml. Each published version is immutable — you cannot overwrite an existing version. To release an update, bump the version in your manifest and publish again.
Package names are namespaced to your GitHub identity. You can only publish packages whose name prefix matches your authenticated GitHub username or organisation.

data:
Search the soropkg registry by name, keyword, or category.
soropkg search <query> [flags]
data.arguments#Arguments: Argument	Required	Description	<query>	Yes	Search term — matches package name, description, and tags
data.flags#Flags: Flag	Description	--category <cat>	Filter by category (e.g. lending, dex, oracle)	--network <net>	Filter to packages available on mainnet, testnet, or futurenet	--json	Output raw JSON
data.example#Example:
soropkg search blend
blend-capital/blend@2.0.1
  Lending protocol core contracts
  Categories: lending, defi
  Networks: mainnet, testnet
blend-capital/backstop@2.0.1
  Backstop module for the Blend protocol
  Categories: lending, defi
  Networks: mainnet, testnet
data.browse-all-packages#Browse all packages:
soropkg search ""
Returns all packages in the registry ordered by most recently published.
data.categories#Categories:
Current categories in the registry:
Category	Description	lending	Lending and borrowing protocols	dex	Decentralised exchanges and AMMs	liquidity	Liquidity management	stablecoin	Stablecoin protocols	oracle	Price oracles	yield	Yield aggregators	rwa	Real-world asset protocols	standard	Stellar standard contracts (token, etc.)

data: soroban.toml is the project manifest — the single source of truth for your package metadata, deployed contract addresses, and dependencies. It lives in the root of your project directory.
data.full-example#Full example:
[package]
name        = "my-org/my-protocol"
version     = "1.0.0"
description = "A brief description of what this protocol does"
license     = "Apache-2.0"
repository  = "https://github.com/my-org/my-protocol"
homepage    = "https://my-protocol.xyz"
keywords    = ["defi", "lending"]
[networks.mainnet]
vault        = "CCVAULTMAINNET..."
pool         = "CCPOOLMAINNET..."
[networks.testnet]
vault        = "CCVAULTTESTNET..."
pool         = "CCPOOLTESTNET..."
[networks.futurenet]
vault        = "CCVAULTFUTURENET..."
[dependencies]
"stellar/token"       = "1.0.0"
"blend-capital/blend" = "^2.0.0"
[dev-dependencies]
"stellar/token" = "1.0.0"
data.package#[package]: Field	Required	Description	name	Yes	org/package — must match your GitHub identity for publishing	version	Yes	Semver version string	description	No	Short description shown in search results	license	No	SPDX license identifier	repository	No	GitHub repository URL	homepage	No	Project website	keywords	No	Array of search tags
data.networksnetwork#[networks.<network>]:
Declare your deployed contract addresses per network. Keys are arbitrary and represent the contracts in your package. Values are C... Stellar contract addresses.
[networks.mainnet]
vault = "CCVAULTMAINNET..."
pool  = "CCPOOLMAINNET..."
Supported networks: mainnet, testnet, futurenet.
data.dependencies#[dependencies]:
Packages your project depends on, keyed by org/name with a semver range as the value.
[dependencies]
"stellar/token"       = "1.0.0"
"blend-capital/blend" = "^2.0.0"
data.dev-dependencies#[dev-dependencies]:
Same format as [dependencies], but only used during development (not included in published packages).
Package names must follow the org/name format. The org portion must match your GitHub username or organisation when publishing.

data: One of Soroban's most powerful features is that contract interfaces are stored directly inside the deployed WASM binary on the blockchain. This means you never need a separate ABI file, and the interface you read is always the canonical, live one.
data.what-is-a-contract-spec#What is a contra:
When you compile a Soroban contract with the #[contractimpl] and #[contracttype] macros, the Rust SDK automatically encodes a machine-readable spec — called contractspecv0 — into a custom section of the WASM binary.This spec describes:
All public functions — their names, argument names, argument types, and return types
All custom types — structs, enums, and union types used by the contract
All error codes — named error variants and their numeric values
data.how-soropkg-reads-specs#How soropkg read:
When you run soropkg inspect or soropkg add, soropkg:
Fetches the live WASM binary from the Stellar RPC for the given contract address
Parses the contractspecv0 custom section from the WASM
Deserialises the XDR-encoded spec entries
Formats them as a human-readable interface (or JSON, or TypeScript types)
This means soropkg always reflects the current deployed state of the contract — not a stale cached version.
data.type-mapping#Type mapping:
Soroban types map to TypeScript as follows:
Soroban type	TypeScript type	i32, u32	number	i64, u64, i128, u128	bigint	bool	boolean	Symbol, String	string	Address	string (Stellar address)	Bytes, BytesN<N>	Buffer	Vec<T>	Array<T>	Map<K, V>	Map<K, V>	Option<T>	T | null	Custom struct	TypeScript interface	Custom enum	TypeScript enum
data.further-reading#Further reading:
Soroban contract spec documentation
XDR spec definition

data: When a package is published to the soropkg registry, the CLI computes and stores a cryptographic hash of the deployed WASM binary. This hash anchors the registry entry to the actual on-chain contract — making it impossible to publish a package that misrepresents what is deployed.
data.how-it-works#How it works:
When you run soropkg publish:
soropkg reads your soroban.toml and finds your declared contract addresses
It fetches the live WASM binary from the Stellar RPC for each address
It computes the SHA-256 hash of the binary
The hash is stored in the registry alongside your package metadata
On every future soropkg add or soropkg inspect, the hash can be re-verified against the live chain
data.why-this-matters#Why this matters:
Without verification, a malicious actor could publish a package that claims to be blend-capital/blend but points to a different contract — a honeypot, a rug, or simply the wrong version.WASM verification provides a trust anchor:
The WASM hash in the registry matches the WASM hash on-chain.
If a contract is upgraded after publishing, soropkg inspect will reflect the new interface, and a new version must be published to update the registry entry.
data.checking-a-hash-manually#Checking a hash:
You can verify a registry entry yourself with soropkg inspect --json:
soropkg inspect CAQQR5SWBXKIGZKPBZDH3KM5GQ5G... --json | jq .wasmHash
# ""
Then cross-reference with the Stellar explorer:
# Stellar Laboratory / Lobstr / StellarExpert all show the WASM hash
# for any contract on the Contract Info page
If a contract is upgraded on-chain after its package was published, the registry WASM hash will no longer match the live contract. This is expected — it signals that the package needs a new version to be published. The soropkg inspect command will always show the current live interface regardless.

data: The soropkg registry is the central index of published Soroban contract packages. It stores package metadata, contract specs, and verified WASM hashes — but not the WASM binaries themselves. Those live on-chain.
data.seed-packages#Seed packages:
soropkg ships with 19+ contracts from the core Stellar ecosystem pre-loaded as seed data. These are available immediately without any publishing step.
Package	Category	Networks	stellar/token	Standard	mainnet, testnet	blend-capital/blend	Lending	mainnet, testnet	blend-capital/backstop	Lending	mainnet, testnet	blend-capital/pool	Lending	mainnet, testnet	soroswap/router	DEX / AMM	mainnet, testnet	soroswap/pair	DEX / AMM	mainnet, testnet	phoenix/multihop	DEX	mainnet, testnet	aquarius/liquidity-pool	Liquidity	mainnet	fxdao/usdt	Stablecoin	mainnet, testnet	reflector/oracle	Oracle	mainnet	yieldblox/protocol	Yield	testnet	centrifuge/pool	RWA	mainnet
data.publishing-a-package#Publishing a packag:
See soropkg publish for the full publishing workflow. In summary:
Authenticate with your GitHub identity
soropkg verifies your deployed WASM matches the declared package version
Metadata and contract spec are stored in the registry
The package becomes searchable and installable
data.package-ownership#Package ownership:
Package names are namespaced to GitHub identities. my-org/my-protocol can only be published by the GitHub user or organisation my-org. This prevents name squatting and impersonation.
The registry itself is open source and part of the soropkg monorepo at packages/registry.

data: soropkg is open source and welcomes contributions of all kinds — bug reports, documentation improvements, new features, and registry additions.
data.repository-structure#Repository structur:
soropkg/
├── packages/
│   ├── core/        # Core logic: WASM parsing, spec reading, type generation
│   ├── cli/         # CLI entry point and command handlers
│   ├── registry/    # Registry server and seed data
│   ├── website/     # Marketing landing page (Next.js)
│   └── docs/        # This documentation site (Nextra)
├── seeds/           # Seed contract data for the registry
└── package.json     # Root workspace
data.adding-a-contract-to-the-registry#Adding:
The fastest way to contribute is to add a Soroban contract package to the seed data.
Find the deployed contract address(es) on StellarExpert
Create a new entry in seeds/ following the existing format
Run soropkg inspect <address> to verify the interface parses correctly
Open a pull request with a description of the protocol
data.reporting-bugs#Reporting bugs:
Open an issue on GitHub with:
soropkg version (soropkg --version)
The command you ran
The full error output
Expected vs actual behaviour
data.make-your-changes-and-commit#Make your c: Follow the existing commit message style — short imperative subject line, no co-authors.
data.open-a-pull-request#Open a pull request:
Describe what you changed and why. Link any relevant issues.
For large features or architectural changes, open an issue first to discuss the approach before investing time in an implementation.

data: This guide walks you through installing soropkg and setting up your first project.
data.requirements#Requirements:
Node.js 18 or higher
npm 8 or higher
A Soroban project (or an existing contract address to inspect)
data.installation#Installation:
Install the CLI globally via npm:
npm install -g soropkg
Verify the installation:
soropkg --version
# soropkg/0.x.x
data.initialise-a-manifest#Initialise a manif:
Run soropkg init inside your project directory. It will ask a few questions and generate a soroban.toml file.
cd my-protocol
soropkg init
? Package name (org/name): my-org/my-protocol
? Version: 1.0.0
? Description: My first Soroban protocol
? License: Apache-2.0
? Repository URL: https://github.com/my-org/my-protocol
✓  Created soroban.toml
data.add-contract-ids-for-each-network#Add co:
Edit the generated soroban.toml to add your deployed contract addresses:
[package]
name        = "my-org/my-protocol"
version     = "1.0.0"
description = "My first Soroban protocol"
license     = "Apache-2.0"
repository  = "https://github.com/my-org/my-protocol"
[networks.mainnet]
vault = "CCVAULTMAINNET..."
[networks.testnet]
vault = "CCVAULTTESTNET..."
data.add-a-dependency#Add a dependency:
Search the registry and add a dependency using semver:
soropkg add stellar/token@1.0.0
soropkg add blend-capital/blend@^2.0.0
Your soroban.toml is updated automatically:
[dependencies]
"stellar/token"       = "1.0.0"
"blend-capital/blend" = "^2.0.0"
data.generate-typescript-clients#Generate Typ:
Generate fully-typed TypeScript bindings for every installed dependency:
soropkg generate
✓  stellar__token.ts
✓  blend_capital__blend.ts
Written to .soroban/
Import and use them directly:
import { Client as TokenClient } from '.soroban/stellar__token'
const token = new TokenClient({ contractId: 'C...' })
const balance = await token.balance({ id: 'GABC...' })
data.networks#Networks:
soropkg supports all three Stellar networks:
Network	Flag	Description	mainnet	--mainnet	Production (default)	testnet	--testnet	Public test network	futurenet	--futurenet	Experimental pre-release network	
Pass the network flag to any command:
soropkg inspect --testnet CABC123...
soropkg add --testnet stellar/token@1.0.0
Run soropkg --help at any time to see all available commands and flags.

data:
soropkg is a package manager for Soroban smart contracts on the Stellar blockchain. Think of it as npm, but for on-chain contracts.It lets you discover, install, publish, and inspect smart contract packages — and generate fully-typed TypeScript clients — all from a single CLI.
npm install -g soropkg
data.why-soropkg#Why soropkg?:
Soroban stores contract interface specs directly inside the deployed WASM binary on-chain. soropkg reads those specs directly from the network, so there are no separate ABI files to manage or keep in sync.It brings the ergonomics of a modern package manager to smart contract development:
No ABI files. Contract interfaces live on-chain. soropkg reads them directly.
Semver dependency management. Declare contract dependencies in a soroban.toml manifest and let soropkg resolve versions.
Type-safe clients. Generate TypeScript bindings for any installed package with one command.
On-chain verification. Published packages are verified against the WASM hash of the actual deployed contract.
data.quick-example#Quick example:
# Initialise a new project
soropkg init
# Add a dependency
soropkg add blend-capital/blend@^2.0.0
# Generate TypeScript clients for all dependencies
soropkg generate
# Inspect any live contract
soropkg inspect CAQQR5SWBXKIGZKPBZDH3KM5GQ5G...
data.where-to-go-next#Where to go next:
Getting Started →	Install soropkg and scaffold your first project	CLI Reference →	Full reference for every command and flag	Concepts →	How on-chain specs, the registry, and WASM verification work	Contributing →	Add contracts to the registry or contribute code	
soropkg is in active development. The registry and CLI are functional, and new features are being added regularly. Contributions are welcome — see the Contributing guide.
